---
title: "Module 5: EEG Basics"
subtitle: "Reading the Brain"
format:
  html:
    code-fold: true
---

::: {.clinical-vignette}
You're a junior doctor on your neurology rotation. The EEG tech rolls in the machine and starts placing electrodes on a patient's scalp. There are 21 of them, in very specific positions. The patient looks nervous. "Does this hurt?" she asks. "Not at all," you say. But you're thinking: *where exactly are those electrodes going, and what will they pick up?*
:::

You now have the signal processing toolkit: you know amplitude and frequency, you can sample without aliasing, filter noise, and decompose signals into frequency components. Time to point those tools at the brain.

EEG — **electroencephalography** — records the electrical activity of the cerebral cortex through electrodes placed on the scalp. The signal you see is the summed postsynaptic potentials of millions of cortical pyramidal neurons, filtered through cerebrospinal fluid, skull, and skin. It's small (microvolts), it's noisy, and it carries clinically vital information.

This module covers the three things every doctor needs to know about EEG: **where the electrodes go**, **what the frequency bands mean**, and **how to recognize basic patterns**.

## Where the Signal Comes From

Before we talk about electrode placement, it's worth pausing on what the scalp electrode is actually measuring. The brain's cortex is full of **pyramidal neurons** — tall, vertically oriented cells whose apical dendrites reach toward the cortical surface. When thousands of these neurons receive excitatory input *at the same time*, the tiny postsynaptic voltages along their dendrites sum into a much larger electrical dipole. That dipole is what your scalp electrode picks up.

Two things matter for EEG to exist at all. First, the neurons must be **aligned** — pyramidal cells are, but interneurons and cerebellar cells are not, which is why those structures don't show up well on scalp EEG. Second, they must fire **synchronously** — a single neuron's PSP is tiny, and by the time it reaches the scalp it's attenuated thousands of times over. Only summed, synchronous activity survives the trip through CSF, skull, and skin.

## The 10-20 System: Where Electrodes Go

The **International 10-20 System** is the standard electrode placement scheme used worldwide. The "10-20" refers to the fact that electrodes are spaced at 10% or 20% intervals along the skull from standard landmarks (nasion, inion, preauricular points).

Every electrode has a name: a letter indicating the brain region, and a number indicating the hemisphere (odd = left, even = right, z = midline).

Click on any electrode below to see what it records.

```{=html}
<script src="https://cdn.plot.ly/plotly-2.35.2.min.js"></script>

<div class="widget-box">
  <div style="position:relative; width:100%; max-width:500px; margin:0 auto;">
    <svg id="head_svg" viewBox="0 0 400 440" style="width:100%; height:auto;">
      <!-- Head outline -->
      <ellipse cx="200" cy="220" rx="170" ry="190" fill="#fef9e7" stroke="#2c3e50" stroke-width="2"/>
      <!-- Nose indicator -->
      <polygon points="200,25 188,55 212,55" fill="#fef9e7" stroke="#2c3e50" stroke-width="2"/>
      <text x="200" y="18" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" fill="#888" font-style="italic">Nose (anterior)</text>
      <!-- Ears -->
      <ellipse cx="28" cy="220" rx="12" ry="28" fill="#fef9e7" stroke="#2c3e50" stroke-width="1.5"/>
      <ellipse cx="372" cy="220" rx="12" ry="28" fill="#fef9e7" stroke="#2c3e50" stroke-width="1.5"/>
      <!-- Midline reference lines (subtle) -->
      <line x1="200" y1="40" x2="200" y2="400" stroke="#ddd" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="4"/>
      <line x1="40" y1="220" x2="360" y2="220" stroke="#ddd" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="4"/>
    </svg>

    <div id="electrode_container" style="position:absolute; top:0; left:0; width:100%; height:100%;"></div>
  </div>

  <div id="electrode_info" style="margin-top:1em; padding:12px; background:#f8f9fa; border-radius:8px; min-height:60px; border:1px solid #ddd;">
    <em style="color:#888;">Click or hover on an electrode to see what brain region it overlies and what it typically records.</em>
  </div>
</div>

<script>
(function() {
  var electrodes = [
    // Frontal (blue)
    {name:'Fp1', x:38, y:18, region:'frontal', color:'#3498db',
     desc:'Left prefrontal. Overlies the anterior frontal lobe. Picks up eye movement artifacts (blinks, lateral eye movements) and frontal brain activity.'},
    {name:'Fp2', x:62, y:18, region:'frontal', color:'#3498db',
     desc:'Right prefrontal. Same as Fp1 but on the right. Eye blink artifacts appear symmetrically at Fp1 and Fp2.'},
    {name:'F3', x:33, y:32, region:'frontal', color:'#3498db',
     desc:'Left frontal. Overlies the lateral frontal cortex. Records frontal beta activity and is relevant for monitoring frontal lobe seizures.'},
    {name:'F4', x:67, y:32, region:'frontal', color:'#3498db',
     desc:'Right frontal. Mirror of F3. Frontal beta activity, cognitive processing, and motor planning.'},
    {name:'F7', x:18, y:32, region:'frontal', color:'#3498db',
     desc:'Left anterior temporal/inferior frontal. Near the boundary with temporal lobe. Relevant for temporal lobe epilepsy evaluation.'},
    {name:'F8', x:82, y:32, region:'frontal', color:'#3498db',
     desc:'Right anterior temporal/inferior frontal. Mirror of F7. Important in temporal lobe seizure localization.'},
    {name:'Fz', x:50, y:30, region:'frontal', color:'#3498db',
     desc:'Midline frontal. Overlies the supplementary motor area. Used in motor studies and as a reference point.'},

    // Central (green)
    {name:'C3', x:30, y:50, region:'central', color:'#27ae60',
     desc:'Left central. Overlies the left sensorimotor cortex (hand area). Records mu rhythm (8-12 Hz) that suppresses with movement of the right hand.'},
    {name:'C4', x:70, y:50, region:'central', color:'#27ae60',
     desc:'Right central. Overlies the right sensorimotor cortex. Mu rhythm here suppresses with left hand movement.'},
    {name:'Cz', x:50, y:50, region:'central', color:'#27ae60',
     desc:'Midline central (vertex). Overlies the longitudinal fissure at the top of the head. Vertex sharp waves appear here during drowsiness — a normal finding.'},

    // Temporal (yellow/gold)
    {name:'T3', x:12, y:50, region:'temporal', color:'#f39c12',
     desc:'Left mid-temporal. Overlies the left temporal lobe. Critical electrode for detecting left temporal lobe seizures. Theta activity here can indicate temporal pathology.'},
    {name:'T4', x:88, y:50, region:'temporal', color:'#f39c12',
     desc:'Right mid-temporal. Mirror of T3. Important for right temporal lobe epilepsy.'},
    {name:'T5', x:18, y:68, region:'temporal', color:'#f39c12',
     desc:'Left posterior temporal. Near the junction of temporal and parietal lobes. Relevant for posterior temporal seizures and language-area monitoring (left hemisphere).'},
    {name:'T6', x:82, y:68, region:'temporal', color:'#f39c12',
     desc:'Right posterior temporal. Mirror of T5. Posterior temporal/parietal junction.'},

    // Parietal (orange)
    {name:'P3', x:33, y:68, region:'parietal', color:'#e67e22',
     desc:'Left parietal. Overlies the left parietal association cortex. Records posterior alpha and is involved in spatial processing and attention.'},
    {name:'P4', x:67, y:68, region:'parietal', color:'#e67e22',
     desc:'Right parietal. Mirror of P3. Parietal association cortex activity.'},
    {name:'Pz', x:50, y:68, region:'parietal', color:'#e67e22',
     desc:'Midline parietal. Used in cognitive event-related potential studies (e.g., the P300 wave appears prominently here).'},

    // Occipital (red)
    {name:'O1', x:38, y:84, region:'occipital', color:'#e74c3c',
     desc:'Left occipital. Overlies the primary visual cortex. THE best place to see the alpha rhythm (8-13 Hz), especially with eyes closed. Alpha blocking (disappearance with eye opening) is most prominent here.'},
    {name:'O2', x:62, y:84, region:'occipital', color:'#e74c3c',
     desc:'Right occipital. Mirror of O1. Alpha rhythm and visual processing. Asymmetry between O1 and O2 alpha can indicate unilateral occipital pathology.'}
  ];

  // Position electrodes as absolute divs overlaid on the SVG
  var container = document.getElementById('electrode_container');
  var infoBox = document.getElementById('electrode_info');

  // Region legend
  var legend = {frontal:'#3498db', central:'#27ae60', temporal:'#f39c12', parietal:'#e67e22', occipital:'#e74c3c'};
  var regionNames = {frontal:'Frontal', central:'Central', temporal:'Temporal', parietal:'Parietal', occipital:'Occipital'};

  electrodes.forEach(function(e) {
    var dot = document.createElement('div');
    dot.style.cssText = 'position:absolute; width:32px; height:32px; border-radius:50%; background:' + e.color +
      '; border:2px solid #fff; box-shadow:0 1px 4px rgba(0,0,0,0.3); cursor:pointer; display:flex; align-items:center; justify-content:center;' +
      ' left:calc(' + e.x + '% - 16px); top:calc(' + e.y + '% - 16px); transition:transform 0.15s, box-shadow 0.15s;' +
      ' font-size:9px; font-weight:700; color:white; text-shadow:0 1px 2px rgba(0,0,0,0.5);';
    dot.textContent = e.name;
    dot.title = e.name;

    dot.addEventListener('mouseenter', function() {
      dot.style.transform = 'scale(1.3)';
      dot.style.boxShadow = '0 2px 8px rgba(0,0,0,0.4)';
      dot.style.zIndex = '10';
      showInfo(e);
    });
    dot.addEventListener('mouseleave', function() {
      dot.style.transform = 'scale(1)';
      dot.style.boxShadow = '0 1px 4px rgba(0,0,0,0.3)';
      dot.style.zIndex = '';
    });
    dot.addEventListener('click', function() { showInfo(e); });

    container.appendChild(dot);
  });

  function showInfo(e) {
    var regionLabel = regionNames[e.region] || e.region;
    infoBox.innerHTML = '<div style="margin-bottom:6px;">' +
      '<span style="display:inline-block; width:14px; height:14px; border-radius:50%; background:' + e.color + '; vertical-align:middle; margin-right:6px;"></span>' +
      '<strong style="font-size:1.1em;">' + e.name + '</strong>' +
      '<span style="color:#888; margin-left:8px; font-size:0.9em;">' + regionLabel + ' region</span>' +
      '</div>' +
      '<div style="color:#333;">' + e.desc + '</div>';
  }

  // Add legend
  var legendDiv = document.createElement('div');
  legendDiv.style.cssText = 'text-align:center; margin-top:8px; font-size:0.85em;';
  var legendHTML = '';
  for (var r in legend) {
    legendHTML += '<span style="display:inline-block; margin:0 8px;">' +
      '<span style="display:inline-block; width:12px; height:12px; border-radius:50%; background:' + legend[r] + '; vertical-align:middle;"></span> ' +
      regionNames[r] + '</span>';
  }
  legendDiv.innerHTML = legendHTML;
  container.parentNode.parentNode.appendChild(legendDiv);
})();
</script>
```

::: {.try-it}
**Experiment:**

- Click **O1** or **O2** (the red dots at the back). These overlie the visual cortex — the best place to see alpha rhythm.
- Click **Fp1** or **Fp2** (the blue dots at the front). These are closest to the eyes — they pick up eye blink artifacts.
- Click **T3** or **T4** (gold dots on the sides). These are critical for detecting temporal lobe seizures — the most common type of epilepsy.
:::

::: {.key-concept}
**Naming convention**: The letter indicates the region (Fp = frontopolar, F = frontal, C = central, T = temporal, P = parietal, O = occipital). **Odd numbers = left hemisphere, even numbers = right hemisphere, z = midline.** So C3 is left central, C4 is right central, Cz is midline central. This system is universal — it's the same in a hospital in Tokyo, Toronto, or Tunis.
:::

## EEG Frequency Bands: The Language of Brain Rhythms

EEG is never a single pure rhythm — it's a mixture of five frequency bands, each with its own name, clinical meaning, and typical amplitude. Toggle the bands on and off below, drag the sliders, and watch a realistic EEG emerge from the sum. Expand the **ⓘ Clinical significance** tab under any band to read the neurologist's version.

```{=html}
<div class="widget-box">
  <div style="text-align:center; margin-bottom:0.8em;">
    <label style="font-weight:600; font-size:0.88em; color:#555;">Clinical scenarios:</label><br>
    <button class="scenario-btn" onclick="window.setScenario('awake')"
            style="padding:6px 14px; margin:3px; border:2px solid #8e44ad; background:#8e44ad; color:white; border-radius:20px; cursor:pointer; font-size:0.85em; font-weight:600;">
      Awake, eyes closed
    </button>
    <button class="scenario-btn" onclick="window.setScenario('drowsy')"
            style="padding:6px 14px; margin:3px; border:2px solid #1abc9c; background:#1abc9c; color:white; border-radius:20px; cursor:pointer; font-size:0.85em; font-weight:600;">
      Drowsy
    </button>
    <button class="scenario-btn" onclick="window.setScenario('deepsleep')"
            style="padding:6px 14px; margin:3px; border:2px solid #2c3e50; background:#2c3e50; color:white; border-radius:20px; cursor:pointer; font-size:0.85em; font-weight:600;">
      Deep sleep (N3)
    </button>
  </div>

  <div id="bands_composite_plot" style="width:100%; height:260px;"></div>

  <div style="text-align:right; font-size:0.85em; color:#555; margin:4px 2px 10px;">
    <label style="cursor:pointer;"><input type="checkbox" id="showComponents"> Show components</label>
  </div>

  <div id="bandRows"></div>
</div>

<script>
(function() {
  function seededRand(s) { return function() { s=(s*16807)%2147483647; return (s-1)/2147483646; }; }

  var sr = 512, dur = 2, N = sr * dur;
  var t = [];
  for (var i = 0; i < N; i++) t.push(i / sr);

  var BANDS = [
    {key:'delta', glyph:'δ', name:'Delta', range:'1–4 Hz', color:'#2c3e50',
     ampMax:200, ampDef:80, fMin:1.0, fMax:4.0, fDef:2.5, fStep:0.1,
     clinical:'Amplitude: 100–200 μV. The slowest and largest EEG waves. <b>Normal:</b> dominant during deep sleep (stage N3). Also seen in infants. <b>Abnormal:</b> persistent delta in a fully awake adult suggests diffuse cerebral dysfunction (encephalopathy, metabolic disturbance, or structural lesion). Focal delta can indicate a localized lesion (tumor, stroke).'},
    {key:'theta', glyph:'θ', name:'Theta', range:'4–8 Hz', color:'#1abc9c',
     ampMax:100, ampDef:40, fMin:4.0, fMax:8.0, fDef:6.0, fStep:0.1,
     clinical:'Amplitude: 50–100 μV. <b>Normal:</b> drowsiness and light sleep. Common in children and adolescents. Temporal theta is normal in the elderly. <b>Abnormal:</b> prominent focal theta in an alert adult suggests regional cerebral dysfunction. Rhythmic temporal theta is sometimes seen at the onset of temporal lobe seizures.'},
    {key:'alpha', glyph:'α', name:'Alpha', range:'8–13 Hz', color:'#8e44ad',
     ampMax:60, ampDef:30, fMin:8.0, fMax:13.0, fDef:10.0, fStep:0.1,
     clinical:'Amplitude: 30–50 μV. The "default rhythm" of the awake, relaxed brain. <b>Where:</b> most prominent over occipital electrodes (O1, O2) with eyes closed. <b>Key feature:</b> alpha blocking — the rhythm disappears when the patient opens their eyes. One of the first things a neurologist checks. <b>Abnormal:</b> absence of alpha, failure to block with eye opening, or >50% amplitude asymmetry between hemispheres.'},
    {key:'beta', glyph:'β', name:'Beta', range:'13–30 Hz', color:'#e67e22',
     ampMax:30, ampDef:10, fMin:13.0, fMax:30.0, fDef:20.0, fStep:0.5,
     clinical:'Amplitude: 10–20 μV. Fast, low-amplitude activity. <b>Normal:</b> alert, active mental states. Prominent over frontal and central regions. <b>Enhanced by:</b> benzodiazepines (a very common EEG finding in sedated patients), barbiturates, anxiety. <b>Clinical pearl:</b> lots of fast beta in a patient on a benzo drip is the drug, not pathology.'},
    {key:'gamma', glyph:'γ', name:'Gamma', range:'>30 Hz', color:'#e74c3c',
     ampMax:15, ampDef:5, fMin:30.0, fMax:50.0, fDef:40.0, fStep:0.5,
     clinical:'Amplitude: 5–10 μV. The fastest brain rhythms. <b>Normal:</b> higher cognitive processing, perception, consciousness. Active area of research. <b>Caveat:</b> very hard to record from scalp EEG — the signal is tiny and easily contaminated by muscle artifact. Most reliable gamma data comes from intracranial recordings.'}
  ];

  var container = document.getElementById('bandRows');
  BANDS.forEach(function(b) {
    var row = document.createElement('div');
    row.style.cssText = 'border-left:4px solid ' + b.color + '; padding:10px 14px; margin:8px 0; background:#fafafa; border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;';
    row.innerHTML =
      '<div style="display:flex; align-items:center; gap:10px; margin-bottom:8px;">' +
        '<input type="checkbox" id="en_' + b.key + '" checked style="width:16px; height:16px; accent-color:' + b.color + '; flex-shrink:0; cursor:pointer;">' +
        '<span style="font-size:1.7em; font-weight:700; color:' + b.color + '; min-width:1.2em; text-align:center; line-height:1;">' + b.glyph + '</span>' +
        '<span style="font-weight:600; font-size:0.95em;">' + b.name + '</span>' +
        '<span style="color:#888; font-size:0.85em;">' + b.range + '</span>' +
      '</div>' +
      '<div style="display:grid; grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr 1fr; gap:12px; font-size:0.82em;">' +
        '<div><label>Amp: <span id="ampV_' + b.key + '" style="color:' + b.color + '; font-weight:700;">' + b.ampDef + '</span> μV</label>' +
          '<input type="range" id="amp_' + b.key + '" min="0" max="' + b.ampMax + '" step="1" value="' + b.ampDef + '" style="width:100%; accent-color:' + b.color + ';"></div>' +
        '<div><label>Freq: <span id="fV_' + b.key + '" style="color:' + b.color + '; font-weight:700;">' + b.fDef.toFixed(1) + '</span> Hz</label>' +
          '<input type="range" id="f_' + b.key + '" min="' + b.fMin + '" max="' + b.fMax + '" step="' + b.fStep + '" value="' + b.fDef + '" style="width:100%; accent-color:' + b.color + ';"></div>' +
        '<div><label>Phase: <span id="pV_' + b.key + '" style="color:' + b.color + '; font-weight:700;">0</span>°</label>' +
          '<input type="range" id="p_' + b.key + '" min="0" max="360" step="5" value="0" style="width:100%; accent-color:' + b.color + ';"></div>' +
      '</div>' +
      '<details style="margin-top:8px; font-size:0.87em;">' +
        '<summary style="cursor:pointer; color:' + b.color + '; font-weight:600;">ⓘ Clinical significance</summary>' +
        '<div style="padding:6px 4px 2px; color:#444; line-height:1.55;">' + b.clinical + '</div>' +
      '</details>';
    container.appendChild(row);
  });

  function readBand(b) {
    return {
      on: document.getElementById('en_' + b.key).checked,
      amp: parseFloat(document.getElementById('amp_' + b.key).value),
      freq: parseFloat(document.getElementById('f_' + b.key).value),
      phase: parseFloat(document.getElementById('p_' + b.key).value)
    };
  }

  function updateLabels() {
    BANDS.forEach(function(b) {
      var s = readBand(b);
      document.getElementById('ampV_' + b.key).textContent = s.amp.toFixed(0);
      document.getElementById('fV_' + b.key).textContent = s.freq.toFixed(1);
      document.getElementById('pV_' + b.key).textContent = s.phase.toFixed(0);
    });
  }

  function genBand(amp, freq, phaseDeg) {
    var rad = phaseDeg * Math.PI / 180;
    var y = new Array(N);
    for (var i = 0; i < N; i++) {
      y[i] = amp * Math.sin(2 * Math.PI * freq * i / sr + rad);
    }
    return y;
  }

  function updatePlot() {
    updateLabels();
    var showComp = document.getElementById('showComponents').checked;
    var states = BANDS.map(readBand);
    var nOn = states.filter(function(s) { return s.on; }).length;

    var composite = new Array(N).fill(0);
    var bandTraces = [];

    BANDS.forEach(function(b, idx) {
      var s = states[idx];
      if (!s.on) return;
      var y = genBand(s.amp, s.freq, s.phase);
      for (var i = 0; i < N; i++) composite[i] += y[i];
      if (showComp) {
        bandTraces.push({
          x: t, y: y, type: 'scatter', mode: 'lines',
          line: {color: b.color, width: 1, dash: 'dot'},
          opacity: 0.5, name: b.glyph + ' ' + b.name, hoverinfo: 'skip'
        });
      }
    });

    // Scale noise with active band count so all-off is silent
    var rand = seededRand(77);
    var noiseAmp = nOn > 0 ? 8 * Math.sqrt(nOn) : 0;
    for (var i = 0; i < N; i++) composite[i] += noiseAmp * (rand() - 0.5);

    var trComp = {
      x: t, y: composite, type: 'scatter', mode: 'lines',
      line: {color: '#2c3e50', width: 1.4}, name: 'EEG',
      hovertemplate: '%{x:.3f} s: %{y:.0f} μV<extra></extra>'
    };

    var layout = {
      title: {text: 'Your EEG — sum of active bands', font: {size: 13, color: '#555'}},
      xaxis: {title: 'Time (s)', range: [0, 1]},
      yaxis: {title: 'μV', range: [-300, 300]},
      margin: {t: 30, b: 40, l: 55, r: 15},
      plot_bgcolor: '#fafafa', paper_bgcolor: '#fafafa',
      showlegend: showComp,
      legend: showComp ? {x: 0.55, y: 1.05, orientation: 'h', font: {size: 10}, bgcolor: 'rgba(255,255,255,0.8)'} : undefined,
      hovermode: 'x unified'
    };

    Plotly.react('bands_composite_plot', bandTraces.concat([trComp]), layout, {responsive: true, displayModeBar: false});
  }

  var PRESETS = {
    awake:     {delta:[5,2.5,0],  theta:[10,6,0], alpha:[40,10,0], beta:[8,20,0], gamma:[3,40,0]},
    drowsy:    {delta:[30,2.5,0], theta:[60,6,0], alpha:[20,10,0], beta:[5,20,0], gamma:[2,40,0]},
    deepsleep: {delta:[180,2,0],  theta:[40,5,0], alpha:[5,10,0],  beta:[3,20,0], gamma:[1,40,0]}
  };

  window.setScenario = function(name) {
    var p = PRESETS[name];
    if (!p) return;
    BANDS.forEach(function(b) {
      var v = p[b.key];
      document.getElementById('en_' + b.key).checked = true;
      document.getElementById('amp_' + b.key).value = v[0];
      document.getElementById('f_' + b.key).value = v[1];
      document.getElementById('p_' + b.key).value = v[2];
    });
    updatePlot();
  };

  BANDS.forEach(function(b) {
    document.getElementById('en_' + b.key).addEventListener('change', updatePlot);
    ['amp_', 'f_', 'p_'].forEach(function(prefix) {
      document.getElementById(prefix + b.key).addEventListener('input', updatePlot);
    });
  });
  document.getElementById('showComponents').addEventListener('change', updatePlot);

  updatePlot();
})();
</script>
```

::: {.try-it}
**Experiment:**

- Start from the default mix and **mute every band except δ Delta**. Crank delta's amplitude. That's what deep sleep looks like — huge, slow waves.
- Now leave only **α Alpha** on at 40 μV. That's the awake, eyes-closed rhythm over the occipital cortex.
- Try the **Deep sleep (N3)** preset, then the **Awake, eyes closed** preset. Same brain, different state, dramatically different signal.
- Toggle **Show components** to see each band as a dimmed dashed trace behind the composite — the building blocks you just summed.
:::

## Alpha Blocking: The First Clinical Test

One of the first things a neurologist checks during an EEG is **alpha blocking** — whether the alpha rhythm disappears when the patient opens their eyes.

Why? Because the alpha rhythm is generated by the thalamo-cortical loop when the visual cortex is "idling." When the eyes open and visual input floods the cortex, the idle rhythm is disrupted and alpha drops out, replaced by faster beta activity. If alpha doesn't block, something may be wrong.

Toggle between eyes-closed and eyes-open conditions below.

```{=html}
<div class="widget-box">
  <div style="text-align:center; margin-bottom:1em;">
    <button id="alpha_eyes_closed_btn" onclick="window.setAlphaCondition('closed')"
            style="padding:8px 20px; margin:3px; border:2px solid #8e44ad; background:#8e44ad; color:white; border-radius:20px; cursor:pointer; font-size:0.95em; font-weight:600;">
      Eyes Closed
    </button>
    <button id="alpha_eyes_open_btn" onclick="window.setAlphaCondition('open')"
            style="padding:8px 20px; margin:3px; border:2px solid #e67e22; background:white; color:#e67e22; border-radius:20px; cursor:pointer; font-size:0.95em; font-weight:600;">
      Eyes Open
    </button>
  </div>

  <div style="display:grid; grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr; gap:10px;">
    <div id="alpha_time_plot" style="width:100%; height:280px;"></div>
    <div id="alpha_freq_plot" style="width:100%; height:280px;"></div>
  </div>

  <div id="alpha_note" style="margin-top:10px; padding:10px; border-radius:8px; font-size:0.92em; background:#f4ecf7; border:1px solid #d2b4de;">
    <b style="color:#8e44ad;">Eyes Closed:</b> Strong alpha rhythm (10 Hz) dominates the occipital recording. The patient is relaxed, visual cortex is "idling."
  </div>
</div>

<script>
(function() {
  function seededRand(s) { return function() { s=(s*16807)%2147483647; return (s-1)/2147483646; }; }

  var sr = 512, dur = 3, N = sr * dur;

  var conditions = {
    closed: {
      components: [{f:10, a:40, label:'Alpha (10 Hz)'}, {f:6, a:12, label:'Theta'}, {f:20, a:5, label:'Beta'}],
      noise: 8,
      note: '<b style="color:#8e44ad;">Eyes Closed:</b> Strong alpha rhythm (10 Hz) dominates the occipital recording. The patient is relaxed, visual cortex is "idling." This is normal.',
      noteStyle: 'background:#f4ecf7; border:1px solid #d2b4de;'
    },
    open: {
      components: [{f:10, a:8, label:'Alpha (suppressed)'}, {f:6, a:8, label:'Theta'}, {f:20, a:15, label:'Beta (20 Hz)'}, {f:25, a:10, label:'Beta'}],
      noise: 10,
      note: '<b style="color:#e67e22;">Eyes Open:</b> Alpha rhythm is <b>suppressed</b> (barely visible). Beta activity has increased — the cortex is now actively processing visual information. This "alpha blocking" is <b>normal and expected</b>. If alpha persisted unchanged, the neurologist would be concerned.',
      noteStyle: 'background:#fef5e7; border:1px solid #f0c27a;'
    }
  };

  function plotCondition(cond) {
    var c = conditions[cond];
    var rand = seededRand(cond === 'closed' ? 42 : 99);

    // Build signal
    var t = [], y = [];
    for (var i = 0; i < N; i++) {
      var ti = i / sr;
      t.push(ti);
      var v = 0;
      c.components.forEach(function(comp) { v += comp.a * Math.sin(2 * Math.PI * comp.f * ti + rand() * 0.5); });
      v += c.noise * (rand() - 0.5) * 2;
      y.push(v);
    }

    // Time domain
    var trT = {
      x: t, y: y, type: 'scatter', mode: 'lines',
      line: {color: cond === 'closed' ? '#8e44ad' : '#e67e22', width: 1.3},
      hovertemplate: '%{x:.3f} s: %{y:.1f} uV<extra></extra>'
    };
    var layT = {
      title: {text: 'EEG (occipital) — Eyes ' + (cond === 'closed' ? 'Closed' : 'Open'), font: {size: 13, color: '#555'}},
      xaxis: {title: 'Time (s)', range: [0, 1.5]},
      yaxis: {title: 'uV', range: [-100, 100]},
      margin: {t: 30, b: 40, l: 50, r: 10},
      plot_bgcolor: '#fafafa', paper_bgcolor: '#fafafa',
      hovermode: 'x unified'
    };
    Plotly.react('alpha_time_plot', [trT], layT, {responsive: true, displayModeBar: false});

    // Spectrum: bar chart of component amplitudes
    var freqs = c.components.map(function(comp) { return comp.f; });
    var amps = c.components.map(function(comp) { return comp.a; });
    var labels = c.components.map(function(comp) { return comp.label; });
    var colors = freqs.map(function(f) {
      if (f <= 4) return '#2c3e50';
      if (f <= 8) return '#1abc9c';
      if (f <= 13) return '#8e44ad';
      return '#e67e22';
    });

    var trF = {
      x: freqs, y: amps, type: 'bar',
      marker: {color: colors},
      text: labels,
      textposition: 'outside',
      hovertemplate: '%{text}<br>%{x} Hz: %{y} uV<extra></extra>',
      width: freqs.map(function() { return 2; })
    };

    // Highlight the alpha region
    var layF = {
      title: {text: 'Power Spectrum', font: {size: 13, color: '#555'}},
      xaxis: {title: 'Frequency (Hz)', range: [0, 35], dtick: 5},
      yaxis: {title: 'Amplitude (uV)', range: [0, 50]},
      margin: {t: 30, b: 40, l: 50, r: 10},
      plot_bgcolor: '#fafafa', paper_bgcolor: '#fafafa',
      shapes: [{
        type: 'rect', x0: 8, x1: 13, y0: 0, y1: 50,
        fillcolor: 'rgba(142,68,173,0.08)', line: {width: 0}
      }],
      annotations: [{
        x: 10.5, y: 48, text: 'Alpha band', showarrow: false,
        font: {size: 10, color: '#8e44ad'}
      }]
    };
    Plotly.react('alpha_freq_plot', [trF], layF, {responsive: true, displayModeBar: false});

    // Update note and button styles
    var noteDiv = document.getElementById('alpha_note');
    noteDiv.innerHTML = c.note;
    noteDiv.style.cssText = 'margin-top:10px; padding:10px; border-radius:8px; font-size:0.92em; ' + c.noteStyle;

    // Button highlighting
    var closedBtn = document.getElementById('alpha_eyes_closed_btn');
    var openBtn = document.getElementById('alpha_eyes_open_btn');
    if (cond === 'closed') {
      closedBtn.style.background = '#8e44ad'; closedBtn.style.color = 'white';
      openBtn.style.background = 'white'; openBtn.style.color = '#e67e22';
    } else {
      openBtn.style.background = '#e67e22'; openBtn.style.color = 'white';
      closedBtn.style.background = 'white'; closedBtn.style.color = '#8e44ad';
    }
  }

  window.setAlphaCondition = function(cond) { plotCondition(cond); };

  // Initialize
  plotCondition('closed');
})();
</script>
```

::: {.key-concept}
**Alpha blocking** is one of the most reliable and clinically important EEG phenomena. When a patient closes their eyes, alpha appears over the occipital regions. When they open their eyes, it disappears. If alpha fails to block with eye opening, it raises concern for cortical dysfunction. If it's absent entirely, consider whether the patient is anxious (beta-dominant), encephalopathic (theta/delta-dominant), or whether it's a technical issue.
:::

::: {.try-it}
**Experiment:**

- Toggle between Eyes Closed and Eyes Open. Watch the alpha peak (10 Hz) shrink dramatically in the spectrum.
- In the time domain, notice how the eyes-closed trace has a clear ~10 Hz rhythm, while the eyes-open trace looks faster and more "noisy" — that's beta activity taking over.
:::

## Putting It Together: What You See on an EEG Report

When you look at a clinical EEG, the report will describe:

1. **Background activity**: Is the dominant posterior rhythm alpha? What's its frequency and symmetry?
2. **Reactivity**: Does it block with eye opening? Does it change with alertness?
3. **Abnormalities**: Any focal slowing (theta/delta in one region)? Any epileptiform discharges (spikes, sharp waves)?
4. **Artifacts**: Eye blinks, muscle, electrode, 60 Hz noise — all the non-brain stuff (covered in Module 6)

You now have the foundation for understanding all four. You know where the electrodes go, what the frequency bands mean, and what alpha blocking looks like.

| Band | Range | Amplitude | Best Location | Clinical Significance |
|------|-------|-----------|---------------|----------------------|
| Delta | 1-4 Hz | 100-200 uV | Diffuse | Normal in deep sleep. Pathological if awake (encephalopathy). |
| Theta | 4-8 Hz | 50-100 uV | Temporal | Normal in drowsiness/children. Focal = possible lesion. |
| Alpha | 8-13 Hz | 30-50 uV | Occipital | Relaxed, eyes closed. Blocks with eye opening. |
| Beta | 13-30 Hz | 10-20 uV | Frontal/Central | Alert state, anxiety, benzodiazepines. |
| Gamma | >30 Hz | 5-10 uV | Variable | Cognitive processing. Often contaminated by muscle artifact. |

## Quiz: Check Your Understanding

```{=html}
<div class="quiz-container">
  <h4>Quiz</h4>

  <div style="margin-bottom: 1.5em;" id="eeg_quiz1">
    <p><strong>Q1:</strong> Which electrode would you focus on to evaluate the alpha rhythm?</p>
    <label style="display:block; padding:4px 0; cursor:pointer;">
      <input type="radio" name="eq1" value="a"> A. Fp1 — left frontopolar
    </label>
    <label style="display:block; padding:4px 0; cursor:pointer;">
      <input type="radio" name="eq1" value="b"> B. C3 — left central
    </label>
    <label style="display:block; padding:4px 0; cursor:pointer;">
      <input type="radio" name="eq1" value="c"> C. O1 or O2 — occipital electrodes
    </label>
    <label style="display:block; padding:4px 0; cursor:pointer;">
      <input type="radio" name="eq1" value="d"> D. T3 — left temporal
    </label>
    <div id="eq1feedback"></div>
  </div>

  <hr>

  <div style="margin-bottom: 1.5em;" id="eeg_quiz2">
    <p><strong>Q2:</strong> A patient's EEG shows continuous high-amplitude delta activity while they are fully awake. Is this normal?</p>
    <label style="display:block; padding:4px 0; cursor:pointer;">
      <input type="radio" name="eq2" value="a"> A. Yes — delta is always normal
    </label>
    <label style="display:block; padding:4px 0; cursor:pointer;">
      <input type="radio" name="eq2" value="b"> B. No — delta in an awake adult suggests diffuse cerebral dysfunction
    </label>
    <label style="display:block; padding:4px 0; cursor:pointer;">
      <input type="radio" name="eq2" value="c"> C. It depends on whether they're anxious
    </label>
    <label style="display:block; padding:4px 0; cursor:pointer;">
      <input type="radio" name="eq2" value="d"> D. Yes — it means the patient is very relaxed
    </label>
    <div id="eq2feedback"></div>
  </div>

  <hr>

  <div style="margin-bottom: 1em;" id="eeg_quiz3">
    <p><strong>Q3:</strong> During an EEG recording, you ask the patient to open their eyes. The alpha rhythm disappears. This is:</p>
    <label style="display:block; padding:4px 0; cursor:pointer;">
      <input type="radio" name="eq3" value="a"> A. Abnormal — the alpha should persist
    </label>
    <label style="display:block; padding:4px 0; cursor:pointer;">
      <input type="radio" name="eq3" value="b"> B. A technical artifact from eye movement
    </label>
    <label style="display:block; padding:4px 0; cursor:pointer;">
      <input type="radio" name="eq3" value="c"> C. Normal — alpha blocking is a healthy cortical response
    </label>
    <label style="display:block; padding:4px 0; cursor:pointer;">
      <input type="radio" name="eq3" value="d"> D. Concerning — you should repeat the recording
    </label>
    <div id="eq3feedback"></div>
  </div>
</div>

<script>
(function() {
  var answers = {
    eq1: {correct: 'c', feedback: {
      a: ['incorrect', 'Fp1 is frontopolar &mdash; it overlies the prefrontal cortex and is most notable for picking up eye blink artifacts, not alpha rhythm.'],
      b: ['incorrect', 'C3 is over the sensorimotor cortex. You might see mu rhythm there (which is alpha-frequency), but the classic posterior alpha rhythm is best seen occipitally.'],
      c: ['correct', 'Correct. The posterior dominant (alpha) rhythm is generated by the thalamo-cortical loop involving the visual cortex. O1 and O2 overlie the occipital lobes and show the strongest alpha activity.'],
      d: ['incorrect', 'T3 overlies the temporal lobe. You would look there for temporal lobe seizure activity, not for alpha rhythm.']
    }},
    eq2: {correct: 'b', feedback: {
      a: ['incorrect', 'Delta is normal in deep sleep and in infants, but continuous delta in a fully awake adult is a red flag for diffuse brain dysfunction.'],
      b: ['correct', 'Correct. Continuous high-amplitude delta in an alert adult suggests diffuse cerebral dysfunction &mdash; think metabolic encephalopathy, diffuse ischemia, medication effects, or other systemic causes. This finding warrants urgent investigation.'],
      c: ['incorrect', 'Anxiety typically produces more <em>beta</em> activity, not delta. Delta in an awake adult is pathological regardless of anxiety level.'],
      d: ['incorrect', 'Relaxation produces <em>alpha</em> rhythm, not delta. Delta waves in an awake adult are never normal.']
    }},
    eq3: {correct: 'c', feedback: {
      a: ['incorrect', 'Actually, the opposite &mdash; alpha <em>should</em> disappear with eye opening. If it persisted, that would be the concerning finding.'],
      b: ['incorrect', 'While eye opening does produce some artifact at Fp1/Fp2, the disappearance of the alpha rhythm itself is a genuine physiological response, not an artifact.'],
      c: ['correct', 'Correct. Alpha blocking is a normal, healthy response. The visual cortex was "idling" with eyes closed (producing alpha), and now it is actively processing visual input (alpha suppressed, beta increases). This is one of the standard reactivity tests in clinical EEG.'],
      d: ['incorrect', 'No need to worry or repeat &mdash; this is exactly what should happen. Alpha blocking is a sign of normal cortical function.']
    }}
  };

  ['eq1','eq2','eq3'].forEach(function(qid) {
    var radios = document.querySelectorAll('input[name="' + qid + '"]');
    radios.forEach(function(r) {
      r.addEventListener('change', function() {
        var fb = answers[qid].feedback[r.value];
        var div = document.getElementById(qid + 'feedback');
        div.className = fb[0] === 'correct' ? 'quiz-feedback-correct' : 'quiz-feedback-incorrect';
        div.innerHTML = (fb[0] === 'correct' ? '&#10003; ' : '&#10007; ') + fb[1];
      });
    });
  });
})();
</script>
```

## Summary

You can now read an EEG — or at least, you know the basics:

1. **The 10-20 System** places electrodes in standardized positions. Letters = brain region, odd = left, even = right, z = midline.
2. **Five frequency bands** (delta, theta, alpha, beta, gamma) each carry distinct clinical meaning. Slower = bigger amplitude, faster = smaller.
3. **Alpha blocking** (disappearance of the posterior alpha rhythm with eye opening) is a fundamental sign of normal cortical reactivity.

But there's a catch. Not everything on that EEG screen comes from the brain. Eye blinks, muscle tension, the patient's heartbeat, a loose electrode, someone's cell phone — all of these can contaminate the recording. Next up: how to tell artifacts from actual brain activity, and why it matters more than you might think.

::: {.module-nav}
[← Module 4: FFT](04_fft.qmd)
[Module 5b: Montages & Dipoles →](05b_montages.qmd)
:::
